mnemonics.title: NODE Structure Components
mnemonics.content: DATA + NEXT: Every node has two parts - what we store (DATA) and where we go next (NEXT pointer). Think D-A-T-A stores the VALUE, N-E-X-T shows the DIRECTION.

mnemonics.title: Insertion at Beginning Steps
mnemonics.content: NEW points to HEAD, then HEAD points to NEW. Order matters: 'New Head First' - make new node's next point to old head before updating head pointer.

mnemonics.title: Deletion Steps
mnemonics.content: SAVE the node to delete, BYPASS by linking previous to next, then FREE the saved memory. Remember: SAVE-BYPASS-FREE.

mnemonics.title: Traversal Process
mnemonics.content: Start at HEAD, VISIT each node, PROCESS the data, MOVE to NEXT using pointer, STOP when NEXT is NULL.

acronyms.expansion: Holds address of the first node in the list
acronyms.usage: Used as the starting point for all operations on the list

acronyms.expansion: Not a Local Object - special pointer value indicating end of list
acronyms.usage: Assigned to the next pointer of the last node to mark the list termination

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - order of insertion vs deletion
acronyms.usage: Describes queue behavior that can be implemented using linked lists

acronyms.expansion: Last In First Out - order of insertion vs deletion
acronyms.usage: Describes stack behavior that can be implemented using linked lists

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle divided into two boxes: left box labeled 'DATA' contains a value (like 5), right box labeled 'NEXT' contains an arrow pointing to another rectangle. The arrow represents the pointer.

Represent the list as a chain of boxes connected by arrows. HEAD is a box outside pointing to the first node. Each node box has data inside. Last node has an arrow pointing to NULL (often drawn as ground symbol or empty).
Draw new node as a box. Show arrow from new node going to current head. Then show head arrow moving to point at new node. The connection changes from old HEAD->Node1 to NEW->Node1.
Show three connected nodes: prev -> toDelete -> next. Draw an X through toDelete. Show prev node's arrow bypassing toDelete and pointing directly to next. This is 'skipping' the deleted node.
Draw NULL as a small box with 'NULL' written inside, or use a grounded symbol (line with perpendicular short line). This signals 'no more nodes' and prevents infinite traversal.

analogies.analogy: Think of a singly linked list as a train where each car (node) has passengers (data) and a coupler (next pointer) connecting to only the next car. You can only go forward through the train. The last car has no coupler (NULL).

Each clue (node) gives you information (data) and tells you where to find the next clue (next pointer). You must follow clues in sequence. Without the next location, you cannot find the treasure - this is like NULL telling you the hunt ends.

A restaurant waitlist is like a linked list. Each party has their name (data) and is told who is next in line after them (next pointer). The host keeps the first party's reference (head). When a party arrives, they are removed from the front.

Imagine a hiking trail marked by ribbon ties on trees. Each ribbon (node) shows the direction to the next ribbon. You can only follow forward. The last tree either has no ribbon or a 'trail ends' sign (NULL). New markers can be added anywhere by just changing which ribbon points where.
